questions.prompt: The sequence of events that make up a story from beginning to end.
questions.explanation: Plot is the structured chain of events that drives a narrative, traditionally diagrammed by German novelist Gustav Freytag (1863) as exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution. In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders (1967), the plot traces Ponyboy and Johnny from a Tulsa drive-in encounter through the killing of Bob Sheldon, the church fire, and the rumble. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests plot tracking through multi-part questions that ask students to identify cause-and-effect relationships across paragraphs, with the 2-point rubric requiring two pieces of textual evidence.

questions.prompt: The moment of greatest tension when the central conflict reaches its peak.
questions.explanation: The climax is the highest-tension moment when the protagonist confronts the central conflict and the outcome becomes inevitable. In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders, the climax occurs when Johnny stabs Bob to save Ponyboy at the fountain; in Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons (1994), the climax is Salamanca's discovery at the bus crash site. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 requires students to distinguish climax from rising action; constructed-response items on the state test award full credit only when students cite the specific moment and explain why tension peaks there.

questions.prompt: The opening section that introduces characters, setting, and background.
questions.explanation: Exposition delivers the foundational who, where, and when of a story, typically in opening chapters. In Jerry Spinelli's Crash (1996), the exposition introduces seventh-grader John 'Crash' Coogan's family, his Pennsylvania suburb, and his football-fueled bullying of Penn Webb. In R.J. Palacio's Wonder (2012), Auggie's opening lines establish his facial difference and his first day at Beecher Prep. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests exposition through inference items; the state rubric rewards students who use opening details to predict later character behavior with at least two pieces of evidence.

questions.prompt: Events after the climax that lead toward the story's end.
questions.explanation: Falling action consists of the consequences that follow the climax and steer the story toward resolution. In Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust (1997), the falling action follows the kitchen fire and Billie Jo's mother's death as Billie Jo runs west then returns to her father; in Walk Two Moons, the falling action covers Sal and her father grieving together. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests falling action through questions asking how post-climax events shape theme; the state's 4-point extended-response rubric rewards explicit connections between consequences and the central message.

questions.prompt: The final outcome where conflicts are settled and the story closes.
questions.explanation: The resolution is the story's conclusion, where conflicts settle and the lasting impact emerges. In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders, the resolution is Ponyboy beginning to write the book that becomes the novel itself, framing his loss of Johnny and Dally as a story worth telling. In Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons, the resolution is Sal accepting her mother's death and returning home with her father. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests resolution analysis on 4-point essays; the state rubric requires students to explain how the resolution reflects the author's central idea, drawing evidence from the final chapters.

questions.prompt: The main character in a story.
questions.explanation: The protagonist is the central character whose decisions and growth drive the narrative. Ponyboy Curtis is the protagonist of S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders (1967); Salamanca Tree Hiddle is the protagonist of Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons (1994); Josh Bell is the protagonist of Kwame Alexander's verse novel The Crossover (2014). NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 requires students to trace how a protagonist changes across a text; the state's 2-point rubric for short-answer items awards full credit only when students provide two pieces of textual evidence showing internal or external change in the central figure.

questions.prompt: The character or force that opposes the protagonist.
questions.explanation: The antagonist creates the central conflict the protagonist must struggle against; the antagonist may be a person, group, force of nature, or internal struggle. In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders, Bob Sheldon and the Socs serve as antagonists; in R.J. Palacio's Wonder (2012), Julian Albans antagonizes Auggie at Beecher Prep; in Jerry Spinelli's Crash, Crash himself initially antagonizes Penn Webb. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 frequently tests antagonist identification; constructed-response items on the state test award points for explaining how the antagonist's motives reveal theme.

questions.prompt: A character who changes significantly across the story.
questions.explanation: A dynamic character undergoes meaningful internal change in beliefs, values, or self-understanding across the narrative. Crash Coogan in Jerry Spinelli's Crash (1996) is the canonical Grade 7 dynamic character: he transforms from a bullying football star into a friend who chooses kindness over winning. Auggie Pullman in R.J. Palacio's Wonder also shows dynamic growth. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 explicitly tests dynamic-character analysis; the state rubric awards full credit when students cite a beginning trait, an ending trait, and the catalyst that caused the change.

questions.prompt: A character who remains essentially the same throughout the story.
questions.explanation: A static character does not undergo meaningful internal change; their values and behaviors stay consistent from start to finish. Dally Winston in S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders is largely static—his hardness and cynicism never soften—which makes his grief over Johnny's death emotionally devastating. Mrs. Pullman in Wonder remains a steady source of love throughout. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests the contrast between static and dynamic characters through compare-and-contrast items; the rubric rewards specific evidence of consistency for static characters paired with evidence of change for dynamic ones.

questions.prompt: The techniques an author uses to reveal a character's traits.
questions.explanation: Characterization is the set of techniques authors use to reveal personality, often summarized by the STEAL acronym (Speech, Thoughts, Effect on others, Actions, Looks). R.J. Palacio characterizes Auggie in Wonder through multiple narrators—Auggie himself, his sister Via, his friend Summer—so readers see him through varied perspectives. Hinton characterizes Ponyboy through his own first-person narration in The Outsiders. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 requires students to analyze characterization techniques; the state's 4-point essay rubric awards full credit only when students cite at least two STEAL categories with textual evidence.

questions.prompt: The time and place where a story occurs.
questions.explanation: Setting includes the historical time period, geographic location, social context, and immediate physical surroundings. S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders is set in mid-1960s Tulsa, Oklahoma, where east-side greasers clash with west-side Socs; Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust (1997) is set in 1930s Dust Bowl Oklahoma, where drought and dust storms shape every page. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests how setting shapes plot and character through inference items; the state's 2-point rubric requires students to cite specific setting details that drive the central conflict or shape a character's choices.

questions.prompt: The feeling or atmosphere a setting creates for readers.
questions.explanation: Mood is the emotional atmosphere a reader experiences—suspenseful, melancholy, hopeful, foreboding—created through setting details, word choice, and pacing. Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust creates an oppressive, mournful mood through repeated images of dust seeping through windows and into food. Alfred Noyes's poem 'The Highwayman' (1906) creates a dark, foreboding mood through midnight setting and 'ghostly galleon' imagery. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 distinguishes mood from tone; the 2-point rubric rewards students who name the mood and cite two sensory or descriptive details that produce it.

questions.prompt: When a hostile setting becomes the antagonist of a story.
questions.explanation: This external conflict pits the protagonist against natural forces, weather, geography, or harsh conditions. In Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust (1997), the Dust Bowl itself is the antagonist as drought and dust storms ravage Billie Jo's family farm. In Alan Gratz's Refugee (2017), the Mediterranean Sea threatens Mahmoud and his family as their boat takes on water. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 frequently tests environment-as-conflict; the state's 4-point rubric awards explicit credit for connecting setting hostility to character transformation through specific textual evidence.

questions.prompt: Vivid descriptive language that appeals to the five senses.
questions.explanation: Imagery is descriptive language that engages the five senses—sight, sound, touch, taste, smell—to make scenes and emotions vivid. Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust is built on imagery: 'the wheat stalks shriveled / where there was once a green ocean.' Alfred Noyes uses imagery in 'The Highwayman' ('the road was a ribbon of moonlight') to set a romantic and foreboding scene. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 directly tests imagery; the 2-point rubric requires students to quote the imagery, identify the sense involved, and explain its emotional impact for full credit.

questions.prompt: The spoken exchanges between characters in a story.
questions.explanation: Dialogue is the direct spoken exchange between two or more characters, set off by quotation marks and tag lines such as 'he said.' In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders, dialogue reveals the slang and tone of 1960s Tulsa teens ('Stay gold, Ponyboy'). Jerry Spinelli uses crisp dialogue in Crash to show how Crash's voice shifts from cruel to kinder by the novel's end. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 explicitly tests dialogue analysis; constructed-response items reward students who quote dialogue and explain how it reveals character or advances the plot for full 2-point credit.

questions.prompt: The distinctive way a narrator or character speaks.
questions.explanation: Voice is the distinctive personality and style of a narrator or character, conveyed through word choice, syntax, rhythm, and attitude. Kwame Alexander's verse novel The Crossover (2014) features Josh Bell's distinctive seventh-grade voice through rap-inflected free verse and basketball metaphors. Karen Hesse uses Billie Jo's spare, plainspoken voice in Out of the Dust to match the deprivation of Dust Bowl life. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 tests voice analysis; the state rubric awards full credit when students cite specific word choices that produce a recognizable speaker identity.

questions.prompt: The pattern of speech showing region, social group, or time.
questions.explanation: Dialect is the patterned speech of a specific region, social class, time period, or community, distinguished by vocabulary, grammar, and pronunciation. S.E. Hinton uses 1960s Oklahoma greaser dialect throughout The Outsiders ('Tuff,' 'rumble,' 'fuzz' for police). In Out of the Dust, Karen Hesse uses Depression-era Oklahoma dialect with omitted articles and rural rhythms. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 tests dialect through context-based vocabulary items; the state rubric rewards students who explain how dialect signals time, place, or social group with two clear textual examples.

questions.prompt: Dialogue not spoken aloud, showing a character's inner thoughts.
questions.explanation: Internal monologue presents a character's unspoken thoughts directly, often in italics or set apart from external dialogue. Sharon Creech uses extended internal monologue in Walk Two Moons (1994) as Salamanca processes memories of her missing mother during a road trip. R.J. Palacio uses internal monologue in Wonder when Auggie describes his own face from inside his head. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 tests internal monologue; the state's 2-point rubric rewards students who quote interior thought and explain how unspoken reflection reveals character motivation or theme.

questions.prompt: A character whose dialogue contrasts with another to highlight traits.
questions.explanation: A foil is a character whose traits contrast sharply with the protagonist to highlight specific qualities through comparison. In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders, Dally Winston serves as a foil to Ponyboy—Dally's hardened cynicism throws Ponyboy's sensitivity into relief. In Jerry Spinelli's Crash, Penn Webb is a foil to Crash himself, his gentleness contrasting Crash's aggression. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests foil relationships through compare-and-contrast questions; the 4-point essay rubric rewards students who identify three specific trait contrasts and explain what each reveals about the protagonist.

questions.prompt: The struggle between opposing forces that drives a plot.
questions.explanation: Conflict is the central tension that propels plot forward, classified as external (against another person, society, nature, or technology) or internal (within the character's mind). Every Grade 7 anchor text turns on clear conflict: Ponyboy versus the Socs and class division in The Outsiders, Sal versus her grief in Walk Two Moons, Auggie versus prejudice in Wonder. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 explicitly tests conflict identification and analysis; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit only when students name the conflict type and explain how it forces character change.

questions.prompt: A direct struggle between two characters or groups.
questions.explanation: Character vs character is the most common external conflict, in which the protagonist directly opposes another individual or group. Ponyboy and the greasers versus Bob Sheldon and the Socs drive S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders; Crash Coogan versus Penn Webb drives Jerry Spinelli's Crash before evolving into friendship. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests character-vs-character conflict frequently on multiple-choice and short-answer items; the 2-point rubric requires both a correct conflict label and one piece of evidence showing the direct opposition between named figures.

questions.prompt: A struggle that takes place within a character's own mind.
questions.explanation: Internal conflict happens inside a character's thoughts, values, or emotions—desire against duty, hope against fear. Salamanca's internal conflict in Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons is her struggle to accept her mother's death; Josh Bell's internal conflict in Kwame Alexander's The Crossover is his envy of his brother Jordan's new relationship. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests internal conflict through inference questions; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students cite a character's thoughts or self-talk as evidence of inner struggle and connect it to a decision.

questions.prompt: Conflict between a character and society's customs or systems.
questions.explanation: This conflict places the protagonist against the laws, customs, or institutions of their community. In Sharon Flake's The Skin I'm In (1998), Maleeka Madison's conflict is with the racist hierarchy of her middle school's beauty standards. In Alan Gratz's Refugee, all three protagonists face character-vs-society conflicts with hostile nations refusing them shelter. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 frequently pairs character-vs-society with historical or social context; the rubric requires students to name the social force and explain how the character resists, accommodates, or is defeated by it.

questions.prompt: Conflict between a character and forces beyond human control.
questions.explanation: Character vs nature pits the protagonist against weather, terrain, wildlife, or natural disasters. In Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust, Billie Jo's life is shaped by drought, dust pneumonia, and grasshopper plagues. In Alan Gratz's Refugee (2017), Isabel's family in 1994 Cuba faces shark-infested seas during a Florida escape. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests character-vs-nature conflict; the 4-point extended-response rubric rewards explicit linkage between natural obstacles and the character's resilience, transformation, or theme of survival.

questions.prompt: The central message about life that a literary work conveys.
questions.explanation: Theme is a universal claim about life, human nature, or society, expressed as a complete sentence rather than a single topic word. The theme of S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders (1967) is that class divisions cause real harm but that empathy can cross them; the theme of R.J. Palacio's Wonder (2012) is that kindness is a choice that creates extraordinary lives. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.2 explicitly tests theme through multiple-choice and 4-point extended-response items; the state rubric requires theme stated as a full sentence with three pieces of textual evidence, not a single word like 'kindness.'

questions.prompt: The broad subject a text examines.
questions.explanation: Topic is the broad subject of a text—stated in one or two words like 'family,' 'identity,' 'prejudice'—distinct from theme, which is the complete-sentence claim about that topic. The topic of Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons is grief; its theme is that grief must be walked through with others, not avoided. The topic of Wonder is appearance; its theme is that what people look like matters less than how they treat others. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.2 tests the topic-versus-theme distinction; students who name only the topic on essay rubrics receive partial credit at most.

questions.prompt: A recurring image or idea that develops theme through repetition.
questions.explanation: A motif is a recurring image, idea, or symbolic element that develops theme across a text. In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders, the motif of sunsets recurs as Ponyboy and Cherry Valance both watch them, suggesting shared humanity across class lines. In Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust, the motif of dust seeping through windows reinforces the theme of inescapable loss. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.2 tests motif tracking through 4-point essays; the state rubric rewards students who identify three or more instances and explain how the repetition deepens the central message.

questions.prompt: A short story's explicit lesson, often stated near the end.
questions.explanation: A moral is an explicit lesson stated or strongly implied at the end of a fable, parable, or didactic tale—Aesop's fables traditionally end with stated morals like 'slow and steady wins the race.' In Mr. Browne's precepts in R.J. Palacio's Wonder, each chapter heading functions as a stated moral ('When given the choice between being right or being kind, choose kind'). NYS Grade 7 ELA items distinguish moral from theme; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit for an accurately stated moral expressed as a generalizable claim that could apply beyond the story.

questions.prompt: How a central message deepens across the course of a text.
questions.explanation: Theme development is how an author advances a central idea through accumulating events, character changes, and symbolic patterns from beginning through climax to resolution. In Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons (1994), the theme that grief must be shared develops as Sal's road trip with her grandparents teaches her to speak her mother's death aloud. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.2 tests theme development through 4-point essays asking students to trace the central message across three text sections; the state rubric requires textual evidence from beginning, middle, and end for full credit.

questions.prompt: A direct comparison without using 'like' or 'as.'
questions.explanation: A metaphor asserts that one thing is another, creating a direct figurative equation: 'her voice was music,' 'time is a thief.' Kwame Alexander uses extended basketball metaphors throughout The Crossover (2014) where life on the court mirrors family relationships. Alfred Noyes uses the famous metaphor 'the road was a ribbon of moonlight' in his 1906 poem 'The Highwayman.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 explicitly tests metaphor identification and effect; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students name both terms of the comparison and explain the deeper meaning created by the juxtaposition.

questions.prompt: A comparison using the words 'like' or 'as.'
questions.explanation: A simile uses 'like' or 'as' to make an explicit comparison between two unlike things, highlighting a shared quality. Alfred Noyes's 'The Highwayman' contains 'his eyes were hollows of madness, his hair like mouldy hay.' Sharon Creech writes in Walk Two Moons (1994) of Sal's mother leaving 'like a leaf in the wind.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 tests simile recognition; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students name the two things compared and explain the implied shared characteristic the comparison emphasizes.

questions.prompt: Giving human qualities to non-human things.
questions.explanation: Personification attributes human emotions, thoughts, or actions to non-human entities such as objects, weather, animals, or abstract ideas. Alfred Noyes personifies the moon ('the moon was a ghostly galleon tossed upon cloudy seas') in 'The Highwayman.' Karen Hesse personifies the dust in Out of the Dust as it 'comes for' the family. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 tests personification through multiple-choice and short-answer items; the state rubric rewards students who identify the non-human subject, the human quality, and explain the effect on mood or theme.

questions.prompt: An expression whose meaning differs from its literal words.
questions.explanation: An idiom is a culturally specific expression whose figurative meaning differs from its literal words—'spill the beans,' 'kick the bucket,' 'cost an arm and a leg.' In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders, the 1960s idiom 'tough as nails' describes greaser attitudes; Sharon Creech uses idioms throughout Walk Two Moons to characterize Sal's grandparents. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards RL.7.4 and L.7.5 both test idiom interpretation; the state's 2-point rubric requires students to give both the literal and figurative meanings, then explain how context supports the intended figurative reading.

questions.prompt: The narrative vantage point from which a story is told.
questions.explanation: Point of view is the narrative vantage point—first person (I), second person (you, rare), third-person limited (he/she, one mind), third-person omniscient (he/she, multiple minds). S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders uses first-person POV through Ponyboy; R.J. Palacio's Wonder rotates through multiple first-person narrators (Auggie, Via, Summer, Jack); Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons uses first-person nested narration. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 explicitly tests POV identification and effect; the state's 2-point rubric rewards students who explain how POV shapes suspense, sympathy, or irony with textual evidence.

questions.prompt: A narrator who uses 'I' or 'we' to tell the story.
questions.explanation: First-person narration uses I, me, we, us pronouns, giving readers direct access to one character's mind but limited knowledge of others. Ponyboy narrates S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders in first person; Salamanca narrates Walk Two Moons; Billie Jo narrates Out of the Dust in first-person verse. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 tests how first-person POV shapes interpretation; constructed-response items award full credit when students cite a moment when the first-person narrator's limited knowledge creates suspense, dramatic irony, or sympathy for the speaker.

questions.prompt: A narrator outside the story who follows one character's thoughts.
questions.explanation: Third-person limited narrates using he/she pronouns but restricts access to one character's interior thoughts and feelings—readers see the world filtered through that single consciousness. Jerry Spinelli's Crash (1996) is told in first person, but Spinelli's Wringer (1997) uses third-person limited tracking Palmer LaRue. Many recent Grade 7 anthology selections appear in third-person limited. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 distinguishes limited from omniscient by asking students to identify whose thoughts are revealed; the state rubric rewards specific textual citation of interior monologue or feelings.

questions.prompt: A narrator who knows the thoughts of all characters.
questions.explanation: Third-person omniscient narration moves freely among multiple characters' inner thoughts and feelings, often with editorial commentary. Alan Gratz uses a near-omniscient structure in Refugee (2017) by rotating among three third-person narratives (Josef in 1939, Isabel in 1994, Mahmoud in 2015) to expose how three crises rhyme across time. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 tests omniscient POV through questions asking how multiple perspectives create dramatic irony; the state's short-answer rubric rewards students who explain how shifts in viewpoint reveal theme or build empathy.

questions.prompt: A narrator whose account readers cannot fully trust.
questions.explanation: An unreliable narrator's account is distorted by limited knowledge, bias, denial, or self-deception, forcing readers to interpret events against the narration. Crash Coogan in the opening chapters of Jerry Spinelli's Crash is partly unreliable—his bullying behavior toward Penn Webb is presented through self-justifying narration that readers must see past. Edgar Allan Poe's 'The Tell-Tale Heart' is the classic unreliable-narrator text. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 tests this concept through inference items; the rubric requires students to cite contradictions between the narrator's claims and observable evidence.

questions.prompt: The author's or narrator's attitude toward the subject.
questions.explanation: Tone is the attitude an author or narrator takes toward the subject, conveyed through word choice, syntax, and detail selection. Karen Hesse's tone in Out of the Dust (1997) is spare, mournful, and resilient; S.E. Hinton's tone in The Outsiders shifts between defiant and tender; Kwame Alexander's tone in The Crossover is rhythmic and competitive. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 distinguishes tone (author's attitude) from mood (reader's feeling); state-test items ask students to identify tone with single descriptive words like 'reverent' or 'mocking,' and the 2-point rubric requires textual evidence.

questions.prompt: The emotional atmosphere a text creates for readers.
questions.explanation: Mood is the emotional atmosphere a reader experiences while reading—somber, hopeful, suspenseful, eerie. Alfred Noyes's 'The Highwayman' creates a foreboding, romantic mood through moonlit imagery and rhythmic gallop; R.J. Palacio creates an empathic, occasionally heartbreaking mood throughout Wonder. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 explicitly tests mood through both multiple-choice and short-answer items; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students name the mood in a single word and cite two sensory or descriptive details from the text that produce it.

questions.prompt: Word choices that carry emotional or evaluative weight.
questions.explanation: Connotation is the emotional or evaluative associations a word carries beyond its dictionary definition—'home' versus 'house,' or 'slender' versus 'skinny' versus 'scrawny.' Sharon Creech distinguishes Sal's tender voice from Phoebe's anxious voice in Walk Two Moons largely through connotation. Sharon Flake uses heavy connotative weight in The Skin I'm In (1998) when characters argue about beauty. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.5 tests connotation through word-choice items; the state's 2-point rubric rewards students who explain why two near-synonyms carry different emotional weight.

questions.prompt: The literal dictionary meaning of a word.
questions.explanation: Denotation is a word's literal, dictionary meaning, distinct from the emotional connotations it accumulates through use. The denotation of 'cheap' is 'inexpensive,' but its connotations are often negative; the denotation of 'thrifty' is similar, but its connotations are positive. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.5 tests the denotation-versus-connotation distinction directly: items provide two near-synonyms and ask students to identify which carries the more positive or negative associations; the state rubric awards full credit when students cite both the literal meaning and the emotional weight.

questions.prompt: How a single word's emotional weight shapes meaning.
questions.explanation: Word choice (or diction) is an author's deliberate selection of specific words to create tone, mood, or meaning. Karen Hesse's word choice in Out of the Dust—spare nouns, no contractions, simple verbs—mirrors the Dust Bowl's deprivation. Alfred Noyes's word choice in 'The Highwayman' ('claret coat,' 'cocked hat,' 'rapier hilt') situates the poem in the 18th-century highwayman tradition. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 tests word-choice analysis through 2-point short answers; the state rubric rewards students who quote the specific word and explain its tonal or thematic effect.

questions.prompt: The repetition of beginning consonant sounds in nearby words.
questions.explanation: Alliteration repeats the initial consonant sound of nearby words ('peter piper picked a peck'), creating rhythm, mood, or memorability. Alfred Noyes opens 'The Highwayman' (1906) with alliteration in 'the wind was a torrent of darkness among the gusty trees.' Edgar Allan Poe uses heavy alliteration in 'Annabel Lee' ('the sounding sea'). NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 tests alliteration recognition; the state's 2-point rubric awards 1 point for naming the device and 1 point for explaining its effect on sound, mood, or emphasis with at least two examples from the text.

questions.prompt: Words whose sounds suggest their meanings.
questions.explanation: Onomatopoeia uses words whose sounds suggest their meanings—'buzz,' 'crash,' 'whisper,' 'thud.' Alfred Noyes uses onomatopoeia in 'The Highwayman' through 'tlot-tlot, tlot-tlot' for the horse's hoofbeats. Kwame Alexander uses onomatopoeic basketball sounds throughout The Crossover (2014) for net-swishes and dribbling. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 tests onomatopoeia recognition; the state's 2-point rubric awards students who identify the onomatopoeic word and explain how the sound effect contributes to mood, characterization, or rhythm in the passage.

questions.prompt: The repetition of vowel sounds in nearby words.
questions.explanation: Assonance repeats vowel sounds within nearby words ('the rain in Spain'), creating subtle rhythmic effect without consonant repetition. Edgar Allan Poe uses heavy assonance in 'Annabel Lee' (1849) with long-e sounds linking 'Lee,' 'sea,' and 'me' to bind grief to setting. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 tests assonance under sound-device items; the state's 2-point rubric rewards students who identify the repeated vowel sound, cite the specific words, and explain how the sound supports the poem's tone, theme, or mood with clear connection to meaning.

questions.prompt: Pairs of lines that end in matching sounds.
questions.explanation: Rhyme is the repetition of matching final sounds in two or more words, especially at line ends; rhyme scheme is the pattern, labeled with letters (ABAB, AABB). Alfred Noyes uses a strict rhyme scheme in 'The Highwayman' to create gallop-like momentum. Edgar Allan Poe uses musical rhyme in 'Annabel Lee' with the haunting refrain of 'sea' and 'Lee.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.5 tests rhyme through short-answer items; the state rubric awards full credit for accurate rhyme-scheme labeling and explanation of the emotional or musical effect produced.

questions.prompt: Repetition of a phrase or line at intervals in a poem.
questions.explanation: A refrain is a phrase or line repeated at regular intervals in a poem or song, often at the end of stanzas, creating emphasis, rhythm, and emotional intensification. Edgar Allan Poe uses 'In her tomb by the side of the sea' as the refrain throughout 'Annabel Lee' (1849) to deepen grief through repetition. Alfred Noyes returns to 'the highwayman came riding' throughout his ballad. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.5 tests refrain analysis; the state's 2-point rubric rewards students who identify the repeated line and explain how repetition intensifies emotion or unifies the poem.

questions.prompt: The most important point an informational text makes.
questions.explanation: The main idea is the most important point an informational text develops—its central claim about the topic. In a Grade 7 informational article about coral-reef bleaching, the main idea might be that rising sea temperatures threaten reef ecosystems. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.2 explicitly tests main-idea identification through multiple-choice items and 2-point short-answer prompts. The state rubric awards full credit when students state the main idea as a complete sentence (not a single topic word) and support it with two specific details drawn from across the passage.

questions.prompt: A specific fact, example, or piece of evidence that supports the main idea.
questions.explanation: A supporting detail is a specific fact, example, statistic, quotation, or anecdote that develops or proves the main idea. In a Grade 7 informational text about the moon, supporting details might include the exact distance from Earth (about 238,855 miles), the date of Apollo 11's landing (July 20, 1969), and Neil Armstrong's recorded words. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.1 tests supporting-detail identification through 2-point short-answer items; the state rubric awards full credit when students cite two specific details and explain how each supports the central claim.

questions.prompt: The broad subject an informational text examines.
questions.explanation: Topic is the broad subject an informational text examines—stated in one or two words like 'climate change,' 'civil rights,' or 'cell biology.' Topic is distinct from main idea, which is the claim about the topic. The topic of a Grade 7 article might be honeybees; the main idea might be that honeybee populations have declined due to pesticide use. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.2 tests the topic-versus-main-idea distinction frequently; the state rubric awards full credit only when students articulate the main idea as a complete claim, not merely the topic.

questions.prompt: A short restatement of the main idea and key supporting details.
questions.explanation: A summary is a brief, objective restatement of an informational text's main idea and most important supporting details, written in the student's own words and excluding personal opinions. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.2 explicitly tests summary writing through 2-point short-answer items and 4-point extended-response prompts. The state rubric awards full credit when summaries state the main idea, include two-to-three key supporting points, exclude minor details, and remain neutral in tone. Plagiarized or opinion-laden summaries receive zero or partial credit per the official rubric.

questions.prompt: How an author's central claim deepens across an entire text.
questions.explanation: Central-idea development is how an author builds and supports a main claim across an entire informational text through accumulating evidence, examples, and reasoning. In a Grade 7 article about the importance of clean water, the central idea develops as the author moves from a personal story to global statistics to policy recommendations. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.2 tests central-idea development through 4-point essays; the state rubric requires students to trace the main idea across three text sections with explicit textual evidence from each section.

questions.prompt: A text structure that examines similarities and differences.
questions.explanation: Compare-and-contrast structure examines similarities and differences between two or more subjects, signaled by transitions like 'similarly,' 'in contrast,' 'unlike,' 'on the other hand,' and 'both.' A Grade 7 article might compare and contrast the school systems of Japan and the United States. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.5 tests this structure through both multiple-choice items and 2-point short answers; the state rubric rewards students who identify the two subjects, name a key similarity and a key difference, and cite transition words that signal the structure.

questions.prompt: A text structure that lists examples or features in any order.
questions.explanation: Descriptive (or list) structure presents features, examples, or characteristics of a topic, often in no required order, signaled by transitions like 'for example,' 'in addition,' 'also,' and 'another feature.' A Grade 7 informational article about deep-sea creatures might list features of giant squid, anglerfish, and octopuses without strict sequence. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.5 tests descriptive structure; the state's 2-point rubric awards credit when students name the structure, identify two listed features, and explain why an unordered list suits the author's purpose.

questions.prompt: A visual that shows information through pictures, charts, or maps.
questions.explanation: A graphic is a visual representation—diagram, chart, map, photograph with caption, infographic, timeline—that supplements text by displaying information visually. Grade 7 informational articles often include graphs showing change over time, maps locating events, and infographics summarizing statistics. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.7 explicitly tests how visuals contribute to understanding; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students describe what the graphic shows and explain how it deepens, clarifies, or complicates the main idea developed in the running text.

questions.prompt: A short note explaining what is happening in an image.
questions.explanation: A caption is a short explanatory text placed beneath or alongside a photograph, diagram, or other image, naming the subject and explaining its relevance to the article. Captions often appear in bold or italic and are essential to interpreting Grade 7 informational graphics. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.7 tests caption analysis on items asking how an image and its caption together support the main idea; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students paraphrase the caption and explain its connection to the surrounding text.

questions.prompt: Bold words a reader can click or look up for more information.
questions.explanation: Hyperlinks are clickable words or phrases in digital informational texts that lead readers to related sources, definitions, or additional context—often visually marked in blue and underlined. In online Grade 7 informational articles from sources like Newsela or PBS LearningMedia, hyperlinks lead to primary sources, related articles, and reference materials. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.7 increasingly tests digital text features; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students explain what additional information a hyperlink would provide and why it would deepen understanding.

questions.prompt: Quoting specific words from a text to support a claim.
questions.explanation: Citing evidence means quoting or paraphrasing specific text from an informational article to support a claim, then explaining the connection in your own words. The NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.1 makes this the foundational reading skill: every short-answer and extended-response item demands evidence-based responses. The state's 2-point short-answer rubric awards 1 point for an accurate claim and 1 point for a specific text-based citation—quotation marks, attribution, or paragraph reference—with explicit explanation of how the evidence supports the claim.

questions.prompt: Restating information in your own words.
questions.explanation: Paraphrasing is restating information in one's own words while preserving the original meaning, distinct from quoting (using exact words) and summarizing (a brief overview). Effective paraphrase requires changing both vocabulary and sentence structure. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.8 tests paraphrasing through evidence-based writing prompts; the state's 4-point essay rubric rewards students who paraphrase sources accurately and clearly, without copying source language, and who give credit through embedded references like 'according to the article' or 'the author explains.'

questions.prompt: A reader's logical guess based on text clues.
questions.explanation: An inference is a logical conclusion a reader draws by combining specific text clues with background knowledge, going beyond what is directly stated. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.1 makes inferential reading a central skill: state-test items often ask students to infer an author's attitude, an unstated cause, or a probable consequence based on textual evidence. The state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students state the inference and cite two specific text clues that justify it—random guesses without textual grounding receive zero points.

questions.prompt: A claim or position an author argues for.
questions.explanation: An argument is the central claim an author makes and supports through reasoning and evidence in an informational or persuasive text. In a Grade 7 op-ed on school start times, the author's argument might be that middle schools should start later because teenagers need more sleep. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.8 explicitly tests argument analysis; the state's 4-point extended-response rubric awards full credit when students state the author's argument as a complete sentence and identify at least two pieces of supporting reasoning or evidence cited in the text.

questions.prompt: A statement that can be proven true or false.
questions.explanation: A fact is a statement that can be verified through observation, measurement, or trusted sources—distinct from opinion, which expresses a personal belief or judgment. 'The moon orbits Earth approximately every 27.3 days' is a fact; 'the moon is beautiful' is an opinion. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.8 tests the fact-versus-opinion distinction directly; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students label two statements correctly (one fact, one opinion) and explain how each could be verified (or why an opinion cannot be).

questions.prompt: The author's deliberate selection of specific words.
questions.explanation: Word choice (diction) is an author's deliberate selection of specific words to create tone, mood, or meaning in informational text. A Grade 7 article about wildfires might use 'devastated' versus 'damaged' to amplify emotional weight, or 'researchers' versus 'scientists' to convey authority. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.4 explicitly tests word-choice analysis; the state's 2-point short-answer rubric awards full credit when students quote a specific word, describe its emotional or rhetorical weight, and explain how it shapes the reader's interpretation of the topic or the author's argument.

questions.prompt: Specialized vocabulary used in a specific subject or field.
questions.explanation: Technical (or domain-specific) vocabulary is the specialized language used within a particular subject or profession—words like 'photosynthesis' in biology, 'tectonic plate' in earth science, or 'tariff' in economics. Grade 7 informational texts in science and social studies are dense with technical vocabulary that readers must decode using context, root words, or glossaries. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.6 tests technical vocabulary; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students define a technical term using context clues and explain why precise vocabulary matters in the discipline.

questions.prompt: Hints about a word's meaning found in surrounding text.
questions.explanation: Context clues are hints about an unknown word's meaning embedded in surrounding sentences—synonyms, antonyms, definitions, examples, or restatements. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards RI.7.4 and L.7.4 both test context-clue use as the primary vocabulary strategy. The state's 2-point short-answer rubric awards full credit when students provide a definition based on context, identify the specific context clue type (synonym, example, etc.), and quote the surrounding sentence that provides the clue—not dictionary lookups, which the test does not allow.

questions.prompt: The reason an author writes a particular text.
questions.explanation: Author's purpose is the reason a writer creates a text—typically remembered through the PIE acronym: persuade, inform, entertain. Author's purpose in a Grade 7 op-ed on screen time might be to persuade parents to set limits; in a science article it might be to inform readers about how vaccines work. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.6 explicitly tests author's-purpose analysis; the state's 2-point short-answer rubric awards full credit when students name the purpose, cite specific text evidence that signals it, and explain how the purpose shapes the author's choices.

questions.prompt: Writing whose purpose is to share facts and explain ideas.
questions.explanation: Informative writing aims to convey factual information and explain ideas clearly, without persuading the reader to take a position. Grade 7 informative articles cover topics like animal behavior, historical events, or scientific processes using evidence, definitions, and examples. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.2 tests informative writing as both a reading skill (RI.7.6) and a writing skill (W.7.2). The state rubric for 4-point informative essays awards full credit for a clear central idea, organized supporting facts and definitions, and a concluding statement that ties the information together.

questions.prompt: Writing whose purpose is to amuse or engage readers emotionally.
questions.explanation: Narrative writing tells a story—real or imagined—with characters, setting, conflict, and plot, often aiming to entertain or convey theme through experience. Grade 7 narrative writing includes personal essays, short stories, and memoir excerpts; published examples include S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders and Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.3 tests narrative writing skills directly; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students develop characters, sequence events with transitions, use dialogue and sensory detail, and produce a clear resolution.

questions.prompt: Specific words or facts an author uses to develop a purpose.
questions.explanation: Supporting details are the specific facts, examples, statistics, quotations, or anecdotes an author uses to develop and prove a central purpose or claim. In a Grade 7 article arguing for stricter recycling laws, supporting details might include statistics on landfill volume, quotations from environmental scientists, and case studies of cities with successful programs. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.1 tests supporting-detail identification through multiple-choice and short-answer items; the state's 2-point rubric requires students to cite specific details and explain how each supports the author's purpose.

questions.prompt: The position or belief an author argues for.
questions.explanation: A claim is the central position or belief an author argues for and supports throughout a persuasive text. In a Grade 7 op-ed arguing that middle schools should start later, the claim is 'middle schools should begin no earlier than 8:30 a.m.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.8 explicitly tests claim identification; the state's 4-point extended-response rubric awards full credit when students state the author's claim as a complete sentence (not as a topic), explain how the author supports it, and evaluate whether the support is logical and adequate.

questions.prompt: Specific facts, examples, or data that support a claim.
questions.explanation: Evidence is the specific facts, examples, statistics, expert quotations, or research data an author uses to prove a claim. Strong Grade 7 argumentative writing distinguishes between relevant evidence (directly tied to the claim) and irrelevant evidence (off-topic information). NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.8 tests evidence evaluation directly; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students cite at least two pieces of specific evidence the author uses, judge whether the evidence is relevant to the claim, and explain why each piece either does or does not support the argument.

questions.prompt: An appeal to readers' emotions to persuade them.
questions.explanation: An emotional appeal (pathos) attempts to persuade readers by stirring feelings—sympathy, anger, fear, hope—often through stories, vivid descriptions, or charged language. In a Grade 7 article advocating for refugee aid, emotional appeals might include personal accounts of family separation. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.8 tests appeal recognition through items asking students to identify when an author uses emotion rather than logic; the state's 2-point short-answer rubric awards full credit when students name the appeal and cite the specific language or story that creates it.

questions.prompt: A one-sided view that favors a particular position.
questions.explanation: Bias is a one-sided view that favors a particular position or group, often shown through selective evidence, loaded language, or omission of counterarguments. A Grade 7 article that quotes only people who agree with its position shows source bias. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.6 tests bias recognition through questions asking students to identify which side an author favors and why; the state's 2-point short-answer rubric awards full credit when students label the bias, cite two pieces of text evidence (loaded language, one-sided quotation, omitted perspective), and explain the effect on readers.

questions.prompt: Choosing only evidence that supports one side.
questions.explanation: Cherry-picking is the dishonest selection of only evidence that supports an author's position while omitting contradicting evidence. A Grade 7 article arguing against video games that ignores studies showing cognitive benefits cherry-picks evidence. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.8 tests evidence evaluation including detection of cherry-picking; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students notice gaps in evidence, name what counterargument the author omits, and explain how the omission weakens the argument's credibility and trustworthiness for a careful reader.

questions.prompt: An argument against the author's main claim.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is an argument that opposes the author's main claim, acknowledged so it can be refuted. Strong Grade 7 argumentative writing acknowledges counterclaims to demonstrate fairness and strengthen the main argument. If the claim is 'middle schools should start later,' a counterclaim is 'later start times create child-care problems for working families.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1 explicitly requires counterclaim acknowledgment in argumentative writing; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students name the counterclaim, refute it with evidence, and return to the main argument.

questions.prompt: The author's response to an opposing argument.
questions.explanation: A rebuttal is the author's response to a counterclaim, explaining why the opposing argument fails or is less important. If the counterclaim is 'later school starts create child-care problems,' a rebuttal might be 'recent research shows after-care programs are cheaper to run than the academic and health costs of sleep-deprived adolescents.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1 tests rebuttal in argumentative writing; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students provide an evidence-based response that directly refutes the counterclaim and ties back to the main argument.

questions.prompt: Acknowledging the strengths of an opposing view.
questions.explanation: Concession is acknowledging the legitimate strength of an opposing argument while still maintaining one's own position—signaled by transitions like 'while it is true that' or 'although critics argue.' Concession strengthens credibility by showing fairness. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1 tests concession in argumentative writing as a maturing rhetorical skill; the state's 4-point essay rubric distinguishes simple opinion essays from full arguments by whether students concede a valid opposing point and then explain why their position still holds despite the concession.

questions.prompt: Reasoning that explains why the counterclaim fails.
questions.explanation: Refutation reasoning is the logical explanation of why a counterclaim is incorrect, irrelevant, or weaker than the main argument. After acknowledging a counterclaim, strong Grade 7 writers explain through reasoning and evidence why their original position still stands. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1 explicitly requires this reasoning move in argumentative writing; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students provide both the rebuttal claim and clear logical reasoning that explains the flaw in the counterclaim, supported by at least one piece of evidence.

questions.prompt: A sentence that signals an opposing view is being introduced.
questions.explanation: Counterclaim signals are transition phrases that introduce opposing views, such as 'critics argue,' 'some readers may say,' 'on the other hand,' 'however,' and 'while it is true that.' These transitions cue readers that the author is about to acknowledge an opposing view, often to refute it. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.c explicitly tests cohesive transitions in argumentative writing; the state's 4-point rubric awards points for clear use of counterclaim and rebuttal signal phrases that guide readers through the rhetorical move from acknowledgment to refutation.

questions.prompt: Combining ideas from multiple sources into a single argument.
questions.explanation: Synthesis is the integration of ideas, evidence, or arguments from multiple sources into a single coherent claim or analysis. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards RI.7.9 and W.7.7 both test synthesis through paired-passage prompts and short research tasks. The state's 4-point extended-response rubric awards full credit when students integrate evidence from both passages, attribute each piece to its source clearly, and produce an integrated claim that goes beyond what either source says alone—the hallmark of synthesis versus simple summary.

questions.prompt: A clear statement of the position you will argue.
questions.explanation: A thesis statement is a clear declarative sentence that states the position the writer will argue throughout an essay. Strong Grade 7 thesis statements appear in the introduction, name the topic, and take a defensible position—for example, 'Middle schools should begin no earlier than 8:30 a.m. because adolescents need more sleep, learn better in late mornings, and face health consequences from early starts.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.a tests thesis development; the state's 4-point essay rubric awards full credit when students place a clear, specific thesis in the introduction.

questions.prompt: The reasons that support a thesis statement.
questions.explanation: Reasons are the logical points a writer makes to support a thesis. A Grade 7 essay arguing for later school start times might use three reasons: adolescent biology requires more sleep, learning improves in late mornings, and teen mental health benefits from rest. Each reason becomes the topic sentence of a body paragraph. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.a explicitly tests reasons that support claims; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students provide at least two clear, distinct reasons in body paragraphs, each developed with evidence and explanation.

questions.prompt: Specific facts or data that prove a reason is valid.
questions.explanation: Evidence is the specific facts, statistics, quotations, examples, or research findings a writer cites to prove that each reason is true. In a Grade 7 essay arguing for later start times, evidence might include American Academy of Pediatrics data showing teen sleep needs, peer-reviewed studies on academic performance, and CDC statistics on teen drowsy-driving accidents. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.b tests evidence use directly; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students cite at least two pieces of specific evidence per reason, attribute the source, and link it explicitly to the reason.

questions.prompt: An anticipated argument against the thesis.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is an opposing argument the writer acknowledges and refutes to demonstrate fairness and strengthen the main argument. In a Grade 7 essay arguing for later start times, the counterclaim might be 'later school starts create child-care problems for working parents.' The writer acknowledges this concern and then refutes it. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.a explicitly requires counterclaim acknowledgment; the state's 4-point rubric distinguishes 2- and 3-point essays (no counterclaim) from full 4-point essays (clear acknowledgment of a real counterclaim with effective refutation).

questions.prompt: A closing paragraph that restates the thesis and broadens the discussion.
questions.explanation: A conclusion is the final paragraph that restates the thesis in fresh language, summarizes the main reasons, and broadens the discussion to its larger significance or a call to action. Strong Grade 7 conclusions go beyond simple summary by connecting the argument to readers' lives or to a wider context. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.e tests conclusion writing; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students restate the thesis without copying the introduction word-for-word, summarize the main reasons, and end with a clear call to action or broader implication.

questions.prompt: The opening paragraph that grabs attention and presents the thesis.
questions.explanation: The introduction is the first paragraph that captures reader interest with a hook, gives any necessary background, and ends with the thesis statement. Strong Grade 7 introductions might open with a surprising statistic, a brief anecdote, a quotation, or a vivid scene. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.a tests introduction writing; the state's 4-point essay rubric awards full credit when students provide an engaging hook, sufficient context for the topic, and a clearly stated thesis that previews the argument or central claim the essay will develop.

questions.prompt: A paragraph that develops one reason supporting the thesis.
questions.explanation: A body paragraph develops one reason or main point supporting the thesis through topic sentence, evidence, and explanation. Strong Grade 7 body paragraphs follow the MEAL structure: Main idea (topic sentence), Evidence (quotations or facts), Analysis (explanation linking evidence to the thesis), Link (transition to the next paragraph). NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.b tests body-paragraph development; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when each body paragraph contains a topic sentence, at least two pieces of evidence, and analysis connecting evidence to the thesis.

questions.prompt: The first sentence of a body paragraph stating its main point.
questions.explanation: A topic sentence is the first sentence of a body paragraph that states the paragraph's main point and previews how it supports the thesis. In a Grade 7 essay arguing for later school starts, a topic sentence might read 'First, adolescent biology demands more sleep than younger children require.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.c tests topic sentences; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students place a clear topic sentence at the start of each body paragraph and develop it with evidence and explanation in the sentences that follow.

questions.prompt: Words and phrases that connect ideas across sentences and paragraphs.
questions.explanation: Transitions are words and phrases that connect ideas across sentences and paragraphs, signaling logical relationships such as addition (also, furthermore), cause and effect (because, therefore), comparison (similarly, in contrast), and sequence (first, finally). NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.c explicitly tests transition use; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students use varied, accurate transitions that signal relationships between ideas, avoiding repetition of basic connectors like 'and then' and overuse of simple sequencers like 'firstly, secondly, thirdly.'

questions.prompt: Sentences that explain how evidence proves a point.
questions.explanation: Analysis is the writer's own explanation of how cited evidence proves the reason and supports the thesis, going beyond merely quoting or summarizing the source. In a Grade 7 essay, analysis might follow a statistic by explaining what it shows and why it matters for the argument. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.b tests analysis development; the state's 4-point rubric distinguishes strong essays by analysis quality—partial-credit essays drop evidence without explanation, while full-credit essays follow each piece of evidence with analytical sentences that link it back to the topic sentence and thesis.

questions.prompt: Writing that explains a topic using facts and details.
questions.explanation: An informative essay explains a topic to readers using facts, definitions, examples, and quotations rather than persuading them to a position. Grade 7 informative essays might explain a historical event, scientific process, or cultural practice. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.2 tests informative writing directly; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students introduce the topic clearly, develop it with relevant facts and examples, use precise language and domain-specific vocabulary, and provide a concluding statement that summarizes or expands the information meaningfully.

questions.prompt: A clear sentence stating what the informative essay will explain.
questions.explanation: The controlling idea in informative writing is a clear sentence in the introduction stating what the essay will explain, similar to a thesis in argumentative writing but without taking a position. A Grade 7 informative essay on photosynthesis might have the controlling idea 'Photosynthesis is the process by which plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to produce energy.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.2.a tests controlling-idea development; the 4-point rubric awards full credit when students provide a clear central focus that the body paragraphs systematically develop.

questions.prompt: A precise definition of a key term in an informative essay.
questions.explanation: A definition is a precise explanation of a key term's meaning, central to informative writing because readers need shared vocabulary to follow the topic. Strong Grade 7 informative essays define unfamiliar terms early, often using context clues, restatements, or formal definitions ('Photosynthesis, the process by which...'). NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.2.d tests definition use directly; the 4-point rubric awards full credit when students provide accurate, accessible definitions of domain-specific vocabulary and integrate them smoothly into their writing rather than stopping the flow with dictionary lookups.

questions.prompt: A concrete instance that illustrates a general idea.
questions.explanation: An example is a concrete instance that illustrates a general idea, making abstract concepts accessible to readers. In a Grade 7 informative essay on adaptation, examples might include the polar bear's white fur, the cactus's water-storing stem, and the giraffe's long neck. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.2.b tests example use; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students integrate at least two specific examples per main point and explain how each example illustrates the general concept being developed.

questions.prompt: A direct quotation from a source.
questions.explanation: A quotation is exact text borrowed from a source and set off with quotation marks, attributed to its author, and explained for readers. In a Grade 7 informative essay on Malala Yousafzai's advocacy, a quotation from her 2013 UN speech—'One child, one teacher, one book, one pen can change the world'—supports the controlling idea. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards W.7.2.b and W.7.8 test quotation use; the 4-point rubric awards full credit when students integrate quotations smoothly, attribute them clearly, and follow each with analysis explaining its relevance.

questions.prompt: Writing that tells a real or imagined story.
questions.explanation: A narrative essay tells a real or imagined story with characters, setting, conflict, and plot, often to convey a theme or significant insight rather than to argue or inform. Grade 7 narratives include personal essays about formative experiences and fictional short stories. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.3 tests narrative writing directly; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students engage readers by establishing a context and point of view, develop characters and events using dialogue and sensory description, use transitions to sequence events, and provide a conclusion that reflects on the experience.

questions.prompt: Description that creates a clear mental image for readers.
questions.explanation: Sensory detail uses precise descriptions that engage the five senses (sight, sound, touch, taste, smell) to create vivid mental images for readers. In a Grade 7 personal narrative about a family meal, sensory details might include the steam rising from soup, the chatter of cousins, the warmth of the kitchen, and the scent of garlic. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.3.d explicitly tests sensory detail in narrative writing; the 4-point rubric awards full credit when students use at least three sensory details per scene that bring the moment to life beyond visual description alone.

questions.prompt: Spoken exchange between characters that reveals personality.
questions.explanation: Dialogue is the direct spoken exchange between characters, set off by quotation marks with tag lines such as 'she said.' Effective Grade 7 narrative dialogue reveals character personality, advances plot, and provides necessary information without resorting to long descriptions. S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders uses sharp dialogue to characterize Ponyboy, Johnny, and Dally as distinct voices. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.3.b tests dialogue use; the 4-point rubric awards full credit when students integrate at least two short dialogue exchanges that reveal character or advance the plot in their narrative.

questions.prompt: Words that signal the order of events in a narrative.
questions.explanation: Narrative transitions are words and phrases that signal the order of events ('first,' 'meanwhile,' 'later that night,' 'years later') or shifts in time or location. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.3.c tests narrative transitions; the state's 4-point rubric distinguishes weak narratives (events stacked together with simple 'and then') from full-credit narratives that use varied transitions to signal pace changes, flashbacks, simultaneous events, and time jumps, making the chronology clear for readers without unnecessary repetition of basic sequencers like firstly and secondly.

questions.prompt: A closing reflection on the meaning of the events told.
questions.explanation: A narrative conclusion reflects on the meaning or significance of the events the story has told, going beyond simply ending the action. In a Grade 7 personal narrative about losing a pet, the conclusion might reflect on what the experience taught the narrator about grief or responsibility. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.3.e tests narrative conclusions; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students step back from the action to reflect on what the events reveal about themselves, others, or a larger insight—avoiding both abrupt endings and overly preachy 'moral of the story' summaries.

questions.prompt: Improving a draft's ideas, organization, and clarity.
questions.explanation: Revision is the process of improving a draft's content, organization, and clarity—adding or cutting evidence, reorganizing paragraphs, sharpening the thesis, and clarifying confusing passages. Revision is bigger than editing, which catches surface errors. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.5 tests revision directly; the state's 4-point rubric distinguishes weakly revised essays (rambling, off-topic paragraphs) from strongly revised essays (clear thesis, focused paragraphs, evidence-based reasoning) and explicitly evaluates whether students have responded to feedback or self-review to strengthen the writing's ideas and structure.

questions.prompt: Correcting errors in spelling, grammar, and punctuation.
questions.explanation: Editing is the process of correcting surface-level errors in spelling, grammar, punctuation, and capitalization that distract readers and undermine credibility. Editing is the final stage before submission, following revision. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.5 tests editing as part of the writing process; the state's 4-point rubric explicitly evaluates conventions, distinguishing essays with frequent surface errors that interfere with comprehension from those whose precise editing makes the writing clear and credible across spelling, punctuation, capitalization, and sentence-level grammar.

questions.prompt: Reading aloud to catch awkward sentences.
questions.explanation: Reading aloud is a revision and editing strategy where writers vocalize their drafts to catch awkward phrasing, run-on sentences, missing words, and unintended repetition that the eye skips over. Many seventh-graders find errors when they read aloud that they miss when reading silently. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.5 includes this strategy among revision techniques; while not explicitly tested, the state's 4-point rubric rewards essays with smooth sentence rhythm and varied syntax—qualities frequently improved by reading drafts aloud to identify and revise problem sentences.

questions.prompt: Feedback from a classmate on a draft.
questions.explanation: Peer review is feedback from classmates on a draft, focusing on what works, what is unclear, and what could be strengthened. Effective Grade 7 peer review uses guided protocols—writers receive specific feedback on the thesis, evidence quality, and organization, not just compliments. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.5 explicitly names peer review as part of the writing process; the state's 4-point rubric recognizes that essays which incorporate clear peer feedback typically achieve stronger development of evidence and tighter organization than essays revised in isolation.

questions.prompt: Looking at how transitions guide readers through an essay.
questions.explanation: A cohesion check reviews whether transitions and references guide readers smoothly through an essay's argument or narrative—ensuring each paragraph connects logically to the next and that pronouns and references stay clear. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.5 includes cohesion as a revision focus; the state's 4-point rubric evaluates cohesion under organization, distinguishing essays where readers must reread to follow the argument from essays where varied transitions, clear references, and logical paragraph order let the argument or narrative unfold without confusion or unnecessary backtracking.

questions.prompt: A word that expresses action or state of being.
questions.explanation: A verb expresses action ('Ponyboy runs') or state of being ('Auggie is brave'); verbs are the engine of every sentence and determine tense, voice, and mood. Grade 7 grammar emphasizes consistent verb tense and subject-verb agreement in writing. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.1 explicitly tests verb forms and consistency; the state's 4-point rubric for writing evaluates verb-tense consistency under conventions, and short-answer items often ask students to identify whether verb shifts within a paragraph are intentional or errors that should be corrected.

questions.prompt: A sentence that asks a question.
questions.explanation: An interrogative sentence asks a question and ends with a question mark: 'Why does Crash bully Penn?' Interrogatives invite reader engagement and often appear as essay hooks, rhetorical questions in arguments, and dialogue in narratives. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.1 tests sentence-type variety; the 2-point rubric rewards students who identify interrogative sentences, name the question word (who, what, where, when, why, how) or auxiliary verb (do, did, can) that opens the question, and explain how rhetorical questions can persuade or engage readers in argumentative or narrative writing.

questions.prompt: A mark used to separate items in a list.
questions.explanation: A comma separates items in a series of three or more (apples, oranges, and bananas), with the final comma before 'and' called the Oxford comma. Commas also separate independent clauses joined by coordinating conjunctions, set off introductory phrases, and enclose nonessential information. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.2.a explicitly tests comma use; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students place commas correctly in lists, after introductory phrases, and around appositives, while avoiding the comma splice error of joining two independent clauses with only a comma.

questions.prompt: A mark that ends a statement.
questions.explanation: A period ends a declarative or imperative sentence and signals a full stop in thought. Periods also follow many abbreviations (Mr., Dr., a.m.). Misuse of periods—particularly in sentence fragments and run-on sentences—accounts for many Grade 7 writing errors. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.2 tests end-punctuation accuracy; the 2-point rubric awards full credit when students place periods at the end of complete sentences and avoid fragments (incomplete sentences ending in periods) and run-ons (multiple complete thoughts joined without periods or appropriate punctuation marks).

questions.prompt: A mark used to combine two related independent clauses.
questions.explanation: A semicolon joins two closely related independent clauses without using a coordinating conjunction: 'Ponyboy reads constantly; he sees himself in books.' Semicolons also separate items in a complex series. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.2 introduces semicolon use; the 2-point rubric awards full credit when students join two complete sentences (each with subject and verb) with a semicolon, ensuring the two ideas relate closely. Misuse occurs when one side is a fragment—semicolons require both sides to be independent clauses that could stand alone as complete sentences.

questions.prompt: Marks used to enclose direct speech or quoted text.
questions.explanation: Quotation marks enclose the exact words of a speaker (in dialogue) or the exact text of a source (in research writing): 'Stay gold, Ponyboy.' Periods and commas go inside closing quotation marks in American English. Quotations longer than 40 words are typically set off as block quotes without quotation marks. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.2 tests quotation-mark conventions; the 2-point rubric awards full credit when students enclose exact spoken words or borrowed text, place ending punctuation correctly, and use single quotation marks for quotes within quotes.

questions.prompt: A mark used to show ownership or contraction.
questions.explanation: An apostrophe shows possession ('Ponyboy's brother') or contraction ('don't' from 'do not'). Singular possessives use apostrophe + s ('the boy's book'); plural possessives ending in s use apostrophe alone ('the boys' books'). NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.2 tests apostrophe use; the 2-point rubric awards full credit when students distinguish possessive 'its' from the contraction 'it's,' form singular and plural possessives correctly, and avoid the common error of using apostrophes to form simple plurals—a frequent mark-down on the state writing assessment.

questions.prompt: Hints in surrounding text that suggest a word's meaning.
questions.explanation: Context clues are hints about an unknown word's meaning embedded in surrounding sentences—synonyms, antonyms, definitions, examples, or restatements. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards RI.7.4 and L.7.4 both establish context clues as the primary vocabulary strategy. The state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students provide a definition based on context, identify the type of clue (synonym, antonym, example, restatement, contrast), and quote the surrounding sentence that provided the clue—dictionary lookups are not permitted on the test, so context is the essential decoding strategy.

questions.prompt: Using a thesaurus or dictionary to verify a word's meaning.
questions.explanation: Reference materials—dictionaries, thesauruses, glossaries, encyclopedias, online reference databases—help readers verify word meanings, find synonyms, check spellings, and clarify subject-specific terminology. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards L.7.4.c and L.7.4.d test reference-material use; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students explain when to consult a dictionary (to verify a definition encountered through context clues), a thesaurus (to find precise synonyms), or a glossary (to look up specialized terms), and demonstrate they can use the reference accurately and efficiently to deepen vocabulary knowledge.

questions.prompt: The emotional weight a word carries beyond its definition.
questions.explanation: Connotation is the emotional or evaluative weight a word carries beyond its dictionary definition—'home' versus 'house,' or 'slender' versus 'skinny' versus 'scrawny' all denote similar things but connote different feelings. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.5.c explicitly tests connotation; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students explain why two near-synonyms carry different emotional weight, name whether each is positive, neutral, or negative, and apply this distinction to evaluate an author's word choices in a Grade 7 informational or literary passage from the test.

questions.prompt: The literal dictionary meaning of a word.
questions.explanation: Denotation is the literal, dictionary meaning of a word, distinct from connotation, the emotional associations it carries through use. The denotation of 'cheap' is 'inexpensive,' but its connotations are often negative; the denotation of 'thrifty' is similar but the connotations are positive. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.5.c tests the denotation-versus-connotation distinction; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students cite both the literal meaning and the emotional weight, and identify which near-synonym an author would choose to create a specific tone in a given passage.

questions.prompt: An author's deliberate selection of specific words.
questions.explanation: Word choice (diction) is an author's deliberate selection of specific words to create tone, mood, or precise meaning.
