Karen Hesse's word choice in Out of the Dust—spare nouns, no contractions, repeated 'dust'—mirrors Dust Bowl deprivation. Sharon Flake's word choice in The Skin I'm In (1998) captures Maleeka's seventh-grade voice with slang and rhythm. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards RL.7.4 and RI.7.4 both test word-choice analysis; the state's 2-point rubric rewards students who quote the specific word, name its connotation, and explain its tonal or thematic effect.

questions.prompt: The 1967 S.E. Hinton novel set among Tulsa greasers and Socs.
questions.explanation: S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders (1967), published when Hinton was nineteen, is a landmark Grade 7 anchor text exploring class division in mid-1960s Tulsa, Oklahoma. Ponyboy Curtis narrates the gang-conflict story alongside Johnny Cade and Dally Winston, ending with Robert Frost's 'Nothing Gold Can Stay' as thematic anchor. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards RL.7.2 and RL.7.3 routinely draw on The Outsiders for theme analysis (loyalty, identity, class) and character development. The state's 4-point extended-response rubric awards full credit when students cite evidence from beginning, middle, and end.

questions.prompt: The 1994 Sharon Creech novel about Salamanca's road trip and grief.
questions.explanation: Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons (1994) won the Newbery Medal and is a foundational Grade 7 anchor text centering on thirteen-year-old Salamanca Tree Hiddle, who travels from Ohio to Idaho with her grandparents while telling them the story of her friend Phoebe Winterbottom—which mirrors her own mother's disappearance. The nested first-person narrative tests RL.7.6 (POV) and RL.7.3 (character development). NYS Grade 7 ELA state-test items often draw on Walk Two Moons for theme of grief and the Native American epigraph 'don't judge a man until you've walked two moons in his moccasins.'

questions.prompt: The 2014 Kwame Alexander verse novel about basketball-playing twins.
questions.explanation: Kwame Alexander's The Crossover (2014) won the Newbery Medal and is a Grade 7 anchor text written in free-verse poetry about twin brothers Josh and Jordan Bell, both basketball stars in seventh grade. The verse-novel form combines basketball metaphors with family drama, climaxing in their father's heart attack. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 frequently tests The Crossover's figurative language and word play, and RL.7.5 tests how the verse-novel structure shapes meaning through line breaks, rhythm, and the rotation of perspective between the twins as a marker of their evolving identities.

questions.prompt: The 2012 R.J. Palacio novel about Auggie's middle school year.
questions.explanation: R.J. Palacio's Wonder (2012) is a Grade 7 anchor novel told through multiple first-person narrators centered on ten-year-old August Pullman, who has a facial difference and is starting fifth grade at Beecher Prep after years of homeschooling. The rotating perspectives (Auggie, Via, Summer, Jack, Justin, Miranda) directly test NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 (point of view). Mr. Browne's precepts ('When given the choice between being right or being kind, choose kind') give the novel an explicit moral framework that supports theme analysis under standard RL.7.2.

questions.prompt: The 1997 Karen Hesse verse novel set in 1930s Dust Bowl Oklahoma.
questions.explanation: Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust (1997) won the Newbery Medal and is a Grade 7 anchor text written in spare free-verse poetry. Billie Jo Kelby narrates her thirteenth and fourteenth years in 1934 Dust Bowl Oklahoma, including the kitchen fire that kills her mother and infant brother and burns her own hands. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards RL.7.4 (word choice and figurative language) and RL.7.5 (structure of verse novels) both draw heavily on Out of the Dust. The state's 4-point rubric awards full credit for theme analysis (forgiveness, resilience, family) with evidence from across the novel.

questions.prompt: The central message about life or human nature in a literary work.
questions.explanation: Theme is the universal claim about life or human nature that a work conveys, stated as a complete sentence rather than a single topic word. The theme of S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders is that class divisions cause real harm but empathy can cross them; the theme of R.J. Palacio's Wonder is that kindness is a choice that creates extraordinary lives. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.2 tests theme through 4-point extended-response prompts; the state rubric requires theme stated as a complete sentence supported by three pieces of textual evidence drawn from beginning, middle, and end of the source text.

questions.prompt: A character who changes meaningfully across the story.
questions.explanation: A dynamic character undergoes meaningful internal change in beliefs, values, or self-understanding. Crash Coogan in Jerry Spinelli's Crash (1996) is the canonical Grade 7 dynamic character—a bully transformed by friendship into someone who chooses kindness. Charles 'Crash' Coogan transforms across the novel through Penn Webb's persistent gentleness. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.3 tests dynamic-character analysis through 2-point short-answer items; the state rubric awards full credit when students cite a specific beginning trait, an ending trait, and the catalyst event that triggered the meaningful internal change.

questions.prompt: The narrative vantage point from which a story is told.
questions.explanation: Point of view (POV) is the narrative vantage point: first person (I), second person (you), third-person limited (he/she, one mind), third-person omniscient (he/she, many minds). S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders uses first-person POV through Ponyboy; Sharon Creech's Walk Two Moons uses nested first-person narration; R.J. Palacio's Wonder rotates among multiple first-person narrators. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.6 explicitly tests POV identification and effect; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students explain how POV shapes suspense, sympathy, or irony with cited evidence.

questions.prompt: Comparison using 'like' or 'as.'
questions.explanation: A simile uses 'like' or 'as' to make an explicit comparison between two unlike things, highlighting a shared quality. Alfred Noyes's 'The Highwayman' (1906) contains the simile 'his eyes were hollows of madness, his hair like mouldy hay.' Sharon Creech writes in Walk Two Moons of Sal's mother leaving 'like a leaf in the wind.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.4 tests simile recognition through both multiple-choice and short-answer items; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students name the two things compared and explain the implied shared characteristic the comparison emphasizes.

questions.prompt: Hints an author provides about future events.
questions.explanation: Foreshadowing is the author's deliberate placement of hints, images, or statements that anticipate later events, building suspense and reader anticipation. In Karen Hesse's Out of the Dust (1997), the early image of dust 'seeping into everything' foreshadows the cumulative damage to Billie Jo's family. In S.E. Hinton's The Outsiders, Johnny carrying a switchblade foreshadows the killing of Bob Sheldon. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RL.7.5 tests foreshadowing through 2-point short-answer items; the state rubric awards full credit when students cite a specific foreshadowing detail and the later event it anticipates.

questions.prompt: The most important point an informational text develops.
questions.explanation: Main idea is the most important point an informational text builds and supports through evidence, examples, and reasoning. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.2 explicitly tests main-idea identification through multiple-choice and 4-point extended-response prompts. The state rubric awards full credit when students state the main idea as a complete sentence (not a single topic word), support it with three pieces of specific textual evidence drawn from across the passage, and explain how each detail relates to the central claim the author develops throughout the article.

questions.prompt: The reason an author writes a particular text.
questions.explanation: Author's purpose is the reason a writer creates a text—persuade, inform, entertain (PIE), often combined in practice. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.6 tests author's-purpose analysis through multiple-choice items and 2-point short-answer prompts. The state's rubric awards full credit when students name the purpose, cite specific text evidence that signals it (such as loaded language for persuade, neutral facts for inform, vivid storytelling for entertain), and explain how the purpose shapes the author's choices about structure, evidence, and language in the specific informational passage.

questions.prompt: The central position an argument essay defends.
questions.explanation: A claim is the central position or belief an author argues for and supports through reasoning and evidence in an argumentative text. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.8 explicitly tests claim identification and evaluation through 4-point extended-response prompts. The state rubric awards full credit when students state the author's claim as a complete sentence (not as a topic word), explain how the author supports it with at least two pieces of evidence and reasoning, and evaluate whether the support is sufficient, relevant, and logically connected to the claim being argued.

questions.prompt: Specific facts or examples that prove a claim.
questions.explanation: Evidence is the specific facts, statistics, expert quotations, examples, or research data an author uses to prove a claim. Strong Grade 7 argumentative reading distinguishes relevant evidence (directly tied to the claim) from irrelevant evidence (off-topic). NYS Grade 7 ELA standard RI.7.8 tests evidence evaluation through 4-point extended-response prompts; the state rubric awards full credit when students cite at least two pieces of specific evidence the author uses, judge whether the evidence is relevant to the claim, and explain why each piece either does or does not support the argument.

questions.prompt: A clear declarative sentence stating the position you will argue.
questions.explanation: A thesis statement is a clear declarative sentence in an argument essay's introduction stating the writer's position and previewing the supporting reasons. Strong Grade 7 thesis statements take defensible positions: 'Middle schools should begin no earlier than 8:30 a.m. because adolescents need more sleep, learn better in late mornings, and face health consequences from early starts.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.a tests thesis development; the state's 4-point essay rubric awards full credit only when students place a clear, specific thesis in the introduction and support it consistently throughout the body.

questions.prompt: An opposing argument that the writer acknowledges and refutes.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is an opposing argument the writer acknowledges and refutes to strengthen the main argument through demonstrated fairness. In a Grade 7 essay arguing for later school starts, the counterclaim might be 'later starts create child-care problems for working parents,' followed by a refutation citing after-school programs and longer-term benefits. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.a explicitly requires counterclaim acknowledgment; the state's 4-point rubric distinguishes 2- and 3-point essays (no counterclaim) from full 4-point essays (clear counterclaim acknowledgment with effective evidence-based refutation).

questions.prompt: A sentence at the start of a body paragraph stating its main point.
questions.explanation: A topic sentence is the first sentence of a body paragraph that states the paragraph's main point and previews how it supports the thesis. In a Grade 7 essay arguing for later school starts, a topic sentence might read 'First, adolescent biology demands more sleep than younger children require.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.c tests topic sentences directly; the state's 4-point essay rubric awards full credit when students place clear topic sentences at the start of each body paragraph and develop them with evidence and explanation in the supporting sentences that follow.

questions.prompt: A direct quotation borrowed from a source.
questions.explanation: A quotation is exact text borrowed from a source, set off with quotation marks, attributed to its author, and followed by analysis. In a Grade 7 essay about social-media policy citing the American Academy of Pediatrics, a quotation might read: 'According to the AAP, adolescents who exceed two hours of screen time daily show measurable sleep deficits.' NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.2.b tests quotation integration; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students integrate quotations smoothly, attribute them clearly, and follow each with analysis explaining relevance.

questions.prompt: A closing paragraph that restates the thesis and broadens the discussion.
questions.explanation: A conclusion is an essay's final paragraph that restates the thesis in fresh language, summarizes the main reasons, and broadens the discussion to its larger significance or call to action. Strong Grade 7 conclusions go beyond simple summary by connecting the argument to readers' lives or wider context. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard W.7.1.e tests conclusion writing directly; the state's 4-point rubric awards full credit when students restate the thesis without copying the introduction word-for-word, summarize the main reasons, and end with a clear call to action or broader implication.

questions.prompt: The emotional weight a word carries beyond its definition.
questions.explanation: Connotation is the emotional or evaluative weight a word carries beyond its dictionary definition—'slender' (positive), 'thin' (neutral), and 'scrawny' (negative) all denote similar things but connote different feelings. NYS Grade 7 ELA standard L.7.5.c tests connotation; the state's 2-point rubric awards full credit when students explain why two near-synonyms carry different emotional weight, name whether each is positive, neutral, or negative, and apply this distinction to evaluate an author's word choices in a Grade 7 informational or literary passage drawn from the test.

questions.prompt: Hints in surrounding text that suggest a word's meaning.
questions.explanation: Context clues are hints about an unknown word's meaning embedded in surrounding sentences—synonyms, antonyms, definitions, examples, restatements, or contrasts. NYS Grade 7 ELA standards RI.7.4 and L.7.4 both make context clues the primary vocabulary strategy. The state's 2-point short-answer rubric awards full credit when students provide a definition based on context, identify the type of clue (synonym, antonym, example, restatement, contrast), and quote the surrounding sentence that provided the clue—dictionary lookups are not permitted during the test, making context decoding essential.
